mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers arithmetic as a branch of mathematics including fundamental definitions, number systems, notation (decimal, binary), divisibility, and logarithms, matching B.1 and all its sub-paths comprehensively.

Broad survey spanning Euclidean, non-Euclidean, projective, analytic, differential, and algebraic geometries, covering section C and its major sub-paths including priority paths C.2, C.3, C.6, and C.7.

Directly maps to section D (Analysis), covering real analysis, complex analysis, functional analysis, harmonic analysis, differential equations, measure theory, and probability — the core content of D.1 through D.6.

Covers elementary algebra, linear algebra, abstract algebra (groups, rings, fields, modules), universal algebra, and category theory, directly mapping to the priority paths B.2 (elementary/multivariate algebra), B.3 (linear algebra), and B.4 (algebraic structures).
Directly covers number theory with all subdivisions: elementary (E.2.a), algebraic (E.2.b), analytic (E.2.c), geometric (E.2.d), and probabilistic (E.2.e) — a precise match to the full E.2 sub-tree.
Lines are foundational objects in Euclidean geometry axioms (C.1.a) and in plane analytic geometry (C.4.a); the article also treats lines in non-Euclidean and projective settings.
Covers 3D Euclidean space as a generalization of geometry (C.1.e), coordinate systems central to solid analytic geometry (C.4.d), and inner product/linear algebra structure (B.3.a).

Directly covers general topology (F.1), topological groups and differential topology (F.2), and algebraic topology (F.3) including fundamental group, homology, cohomology — the full content of section F.
Exponentiation is part of arithmetic calculation (B.1.d roots and powers), connects to logarithms (B.1.e), and extends to group and ring theory (B.4.b, B.4.c) for abstract exponentiation.

Directly addresses the foundations of set theory including fundamental set concepts (A.2.a), Cantorian set theory (A.2.b), and ZFC axiomatic set theory with its postulates and limitations (A.3.a, A.3.b, A.3.c).
Integers are the foundational objects of arithmetic (B.1.a defines natural numbers and integers, B.1.b divisibility) and elementary number theory (E.2.a properties of whole numbers/integers).

Natural numbers underlie the fundamental definitions and laws of arithmetic (B.1.a) and elementary number theory (E.2.a); the article covers Peano axioms and set-theoretic constructions foundational to both paths.

Abstract algebra directly covers all algebraic structures listed under B.4: lattices (B.4.a), groups (B.4.b), fields (B.4.c), rings (B.4.d), categories (B.4.e), homological algebra (B.4.f), and universal algebra (B.4.g).

Linear algebra directly covers all the priority paths: vector spaces (B.3.i), matrices (B.3.i.ii), linear transformations (B.3.i.iii), linear functionals (B.3.i.iv), inner products (B.3.v), and self-adjoint operators and spectral theorem (B.3.v.vi).

Surveys number systems (natural, integer, rational, real, complex, p-adic, transfinite), numeral systems and notation (B.1.c), number properties foundational to real analysis (D.1.b) and number theory (E.2).

Gödel's incompleteness theorems directly address the limitations of axiomatic set theory (A.3.b) and the profound changes and present status of axiomatic set theory resulting from recent discoveries (A.3.c).

Prime numbers are the central object of elementary number theory (E.2.a divisibility and factorization) and analytic number theory (E.2.c prime number theorem, Riemann hypothesis, distribution of primes).

Real numbers are the foundational objects of real analysis (D.1), covering their formal definition via Dedekind cuts and Cauchy sequences (D.1.a origins and concepts) and number system properties (D.1.b).

Zero is the additive identity central to fundamental arithmetic definitions (B.1.a), and its role as positional placeholder is essential to number systems and notation (B.1.c) including the historical development of decimal notation.
Functions are central to differential calculus (D.1.c), functional analysis (D.4), and form the morphisms of category theory (B.4.e); they underpin virtually all of analysis (section D).
Traces the origins of set theory, arithmetic, geometry, and analysis, providing contextual coverage of introductory paths across multiple branches including A.1, B.1.a, C.1, and D.1.a.
Fractions arise directly from the theory of divisors and extension to non-integers (B.1.b) and are involved in arithmetic calculation with decimals (B.1.d).
Solving equations is the principal problem of elementary algebra (B.2.d); the article also covers differential equations, connecting to D.3 (ordinary and partial differential equations).
Area connects to the measure of polygons and the theories of equivalence (C.1.b) and to integral calculus and Lebesgue measure (D.1.d) as the analytic generalization of geometric area.

Euclid's Elements established axiomatic foundations of geometry (C.1.a), the measure of polygons and polyhedra (C.1.b), and geometric constructions (C.1.d); he is the foundational figure for all of C.1.

Gauss contributed foundational results in non-Euclidean geometry (C.2), differential geometry and Gaussian curvature (C.6), and number theory including quadratic reciprocity (E.2, E.2.b algebraic number theory).
Limits are the foundational concept of real analysis (D.1.a origins and concepts), define derivatives and continuity (D.1.c), and underlie the theory of series (D.5.a).
Complex numbers are the domain of complex analysis (D.2), underlie complex projective geometry as homogeneous coordinates (C.3.c), and form an algebraically closed field in abstract algebra (B.4.c).
Euler founded or decisively advanced analysis (D), complex analysis (D.2), number theory (E.2), and topology (F.1 via the polyhedron formula); his breadth spans the major branches of section 10-22.

Noether's foundational work in abstract algebra — ideals, rings, modules, and chain conditions — directly maps to algebraic structures (B.4), particularly groups (B.4.b), fields (B.4.c), and rings (B.4.d).

Mathematical proof is the method underlying axiomatic set theory (A.3) and its postulates (A.3.a); Gödel's incompleteness, which limits proof, is addressed at A.3.b (limitations of axiomatic set theory).
Mathematical series directly covers the theory of series (D.5.a), Fourier series (D.5.b), and harmonic analysis (D.5.c), as well as convergence concepts foundational to real analysis (D.1).
Logarithms are explicitly listed at B.1.e as a sub-topic of arithmetic: their formal definition and use to reduce multiplication/division to addition/subtraction — a direct and precise match.

Angles are fundamental to trigonometry (C.4.b), Euclidean geometry (C.1), and hyperbolic trigonometry (C.2.d); the article covers degree/radian measurement, trigonometric connections, and spherical/hyperbolic geometry.

Trigonometry is explicitly listed at C.4.b; it connects to plane analytic geometry (C.4.a), coordinate transformations (C.4.c), and ultimately Fourier analysis (D.5) through the periodic character of trigonometric functions.

Polygons are core objects of Euclidean plane geometry (C.1), central to the measure of polygons and equivalence theories (C.1.b), and connect to combinatorial geometry via triangulation and tiling (E.1.c).

Triangles are foundational to Euclidean geometry (C.1), the measure of polygons (C.1.b), and the primary objects of trigonometry (C.4.b); transformation geometry (C.1.c congruence and similarity) also applies directly.
Infinity is central to Cantorian set theory and multiple infinite cardinalities (A.2.b), axiomatic set theory (A.3 axiom of infinity), and the limit and convergence concepts of real analysis (D.1).
Volume is part of the measure of polyhedra (C.1.b) and is computed via integral calculus including Lebesgue measure (D.1.d); a moderate but genuine overlap with these two paths.
The overarching mathematics article covers all major branches of section 10-22 — set theory, algebra, geometry, analysis, combinatorics/number theory, and topology — at the parent-path level.

Pi is defined geometrically (circle circumference, C.1), appears in trigonometry (C.4.b), Fourier analysis (D.5), and analytic number theory through the Riemann zeta function and prime counting (E.2.c).

Probability theory is explicitly listed at D.6 as a branch of analysis; the article covers Kolmogorov's axiomatic approach and finite and infinite dimensional probability spaces, matching D.6.a, D.6.b, and D.6.c precisely.

Al-Khwarizmi founded algebra as a systematic discipline (B.2, B.2.a algebra as extension of arithmetic) and introduced the decimal positional number system to Western mathematics (B.1.c number systems and notation).

The circle is a fundamental object of Euclidean geometry (C.1), plane analytic geometry (C.4.a), a named curve studied in mathematics (C.4.e), and the 1-sphere fundamental to algebraic topology (F.3).
Descartes founded analytic geometry and the Cartesian coordinate system, directly corresponding to plane analytic geometry (C.4.a) and coordinates and transformation of coordinates (C.4.c).
Variables are central to elementary and multivariate algebra (B.2), introduced as an extension and generalization of arithmetic (B.2.a) and underlying the basic algebraic properties covered at B.2.b.
